And the answer obviously is yes. And so I thought I'd give a couple examples. You know, an example that I'm going to write about soon because I took a picture of it, which was really interesting to me. I was driving behind a truck. It was one of these large construction type vehicles that clearly had some stuff in it. And on the back of the truck was a large sign that said stay back 300 feet. And I thought this is not a good story because when you're driving on a highway at 55 miles an hour, it is impossible to judge what 300 feet are. I thought I don't know what that is. Like that is a terrible way to tell the story, which is in feet when I am driving in a car that is going 55 miles an hour or more. A better story would have been to say stay eight car lengths behind because at least I could judge approximately what eight car lengths are, although even eight seems like kind of a big number to me. Actually, I think the best sign would have been like, stay the hell away. Things fall out of this truck. And then I think I would have spent like 12 car lengths back, but 300 feet made no sense whatsoever. So that was bad storytelling. That was a failure to tell an excellent story. The other example I'll give you is an example of excellent and bad storytelling simultaneously. because the order in which they have listed the ingredients is really fantastic. Then when I got inside, I had my son who was being weird. He didn't want to have ice cream. And so he wanted something else. It was clear that they just continued to add items randomly and not really assign them categories in any way, not feature anything. And being a person who ran a restaurant for 10 years, one of the things I know is when I'm telling a story on a menu board, the items that are the most profitable to me will be the items that are most prominently displayed. And they will be in such a way that I find them quickly with my eye, which is why in grocery stores, products spend money, product companies spend money to put products on the shelves at eye level. They spend extra money to put their products there. And why when I published my novels in England, they actually asked me to switch my last name. So I am aware of these strategies in terms of telling stories and menu boards and pricing. This store did none of this. This ice cream shop had everything randomly placed everywhere. ## [06:06 - 09:12]

over her stories. My wife has a really refined ear for over rehearsed stories. There's is something inauthentic and lacking in vulnerability when a storyteller does that. So yeah, you can over rehearse. I mean, I don't think my means of rehearsing is going to be very helpful to anyone in particular, but my primary rehearsal locations are in the shower while driving the car while riding my bike. I tend to do those things while speaking out loud. I believe if this is helpful to anyone, no matter what kind of a speaking engagement you are about to do, whether it's a keynote at a big conference center or a toasted wedding or a story at the moth, whatever you're doing, I believe you must rehearse out loud. I know through science that when you speak something out loud, you engage your brain in a more meaningful way and improve your retention, which is why I have kids in school practice their multiplication facts out loud because it is far more effective to be saying six times eight is 48 out loud than just looking at the six times the eight is 48 on the page. Sitting on the page, there is some retention hopefully taking place, but actually speaking those words out loud improves retention considerably. So saying my story out loud, essentially telling myself my own story, whether that is a speech I'm delivering, a toast, a eulogy, anything that I have to speak out loud to human beings, I'm going to be speaking out loud to myself first, and that can be really helpful. impactful as the stories that we tell on stages at the moth or keynotes or any kind of talk that we're giving. In terms of fast forward, typically

## [18:22 - 21:23]

the solution to a fast forward problem is a different chronological structure. So usually when someone's doing a fast forward, they're telling a story chronologically, meaning in time order, ABC. And so they tell something from their childhood. And then they say fast forward 15 years and whatever's happened in their childhood is now echoing in their life. Or, you know, the thing that happened, the predicate of their childhood is now becoming something meaningful in their adulthood. And I say, don't tell it in a ABC order. Tell that in a B ABC or a C ABC order, meaning start in the present and allow the past to be backstory. So in the midst of this present moment, I'm going to pause the story and say 15 years ago, I was doing this as a child, I'm actually working on a story quite literally right now that does exactly this. I opened the story by saying, I'm holding an envelope in my hand. It's a yellow envelope. I have been waiting 42 years to arrive. It has finally arrived to the US mail to my home. 42 years in the making, I now have it in my hand, and I cannot wait to open it. I first started wishing for this envelope 42 years ago. So that that is my way to return to the past by indicating to my audience, this is a story about me standing in a kitchen holding an envelope. And while I'm standing in the kitchen holding the envelope, I am reflecting back upon the past when I first wanted this envelope. And then the tricky point will be, I will talk about the past and why I wanted this envelope and what it meant would have meant to me and what events took place that caused me to want this envelope to arrive. And then rather than saying fast forward 15 years, I'm going to say something like, and so now I'm standing in the kitchen with the envelope at last. And that way I'm not asking the audience to sort of discard 15 years. Fast forward 15 years feels to me like 15 years happened, but I'm not going to tell you anything about them. That feels like pretty, pretty tough in terms of an audience just accepting the fact that 15 years go by, but everything that happens is pretty irrelevant. Please pretend they don't really matter. There are 15 vanilla years and we know that's not the case. So that is almost always how I handle that situation, which is switch the chronology and use the stuff that would have been fast forwarded as backstory instead. In other words, make it a reflection that you are experiencing in the moment rather than an actual moment that is occurring in the story. Forest Gump is the perfect example of this. He's sitting on the bench at the beginning of the movie. He's a 45 year old man. And over the course of the movie, while sitting on the bench, people are going to sit beside him. He's going to offer them some chocolate and then he's going to tell them about his past. He's going to thrust them back into Vietnam. He's going to thrust them back into Chinese ping pong. You know, he's going to thrust them back into his childhood and then always return

## [21:23 - 24:24]

to the bench. And the only reason we returned again and again is because it's too long to go back into the past for that period of time. He has to cover 45 years essentially. So that's why we need different people on the bench so we can go back and forth a little bit. But in most stories, we're not covering 45 years of our life with any detail like Forest Gump is. But that's why they do it there, too. So think about that movie and that structure whenever you have a fast forward. It really means you're probably telling a present tense story, meaning a story that happened more recently and then using the past as backstory. Does that make any sense, though, Susan? It does. It does make sense. I was actually wondering about that today when I started to question the whole fast forward thing. So yes, it makes a lot of sense. And the Forest Gump example was a good one. Yeah, I'll tell you that. You know, the usual suspects is another good example of that. A guy sitting with a guy telling a story about the past and it ends up peas the guy. There's lots of movies that do this, which is they start in the present and the past becomes an enormous part of the movie. The one thing you have to be careful for it is actually something I was thinking about today. I was working on in this story in my shower, going back to that original question, when do you work on your stories in the shower? I was working on this story. And the tricky thing is so often when people use this B A B C, as I call it, or C A B C, meaning we start at the end, then we go all the way back to the beginning, is they don't spend enough time with that first B. You know, I realized when I first told that story aloud to myself, I said, I'm standing in the kitchen holding an envelope that I've been waiting for for 42 years. I first wanted this envelope, and now I'm already back in the past. I don't give them a chance to settle in in the kitchen and the envelope in the present. And people do that all the time. They make that same mistake that I made in the shower today. So I knew in my head, I have to say like 10 sentences, I have to really land them in the kitchen with me as a 50 year old guy holding an envelope he's been waiting for for four decades. I need to make sure that I cement that idea in their heads so that when I come back to it, they're like, of course, we're back in the kitchen now, right? We were there a little while ago, now we're back in the kitchen, he's got the envelope, but they have to return to familiarity. So often if you only give them two sentences, when you return to the present, it's not familiar to them in any way. And they're kind of confused, like, what do you mean you're standing in the kitchen? Because you've given them so little to hold on to in the beginning of the story. When you come back to it, it doesn't feel like anything that they were in before. They've sort of forgotten what you said, which often happens if you don't land an opening scene clearly enough. It's also why we don't open stories with quotes or ideas like there's two kinds of people in the world. There's people who do this and people do that. And then they start the story. Nobody remembers those things. When people open stories with quotes, no one ever remembers the quote at the end of the story. They remember the story. You know, people think they're clever by putting a quote. Even in my own books, I always put epilogues at the beginning of chapters. There's no way anyone remembers the epilogues. They're almost just there for me. They're there because I love the quote and I think it symbolizes the chapter. But by the time
